title: How Does the Discriminator Learn?
concept: The discriminator is trained as an ordinary binary classifier using Binary Cross-Entropy loss, comparing its real-vs-fake probability against the true label of each image.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a GAN, what is the discriminator's basic job?

options.text: Look at an image and output a probability that it's real
Generate new fake images out of random noise
Sort images into ten different object categories

exercises.explain: Correct! The discriminator is a binary classifier: real or fake.

cards.title: One number, one verdict
cards.body: The discriminator doesn't do anything fancy — it looks at a single image and spits out one number: how real does this look? Everything about how it learns comes down to grading that one number.

cards.title: The Discriminator's Loss
cards.body: The discriminator is trained as an ordinary binary classifier. It outputs a probability p that an image is real, and its loss is the very same Binary Cross-Entropy formula you already know: a tiny penalty when p matches the true label, a penalty that explodes when it is confidently wrong.

cards.title: The bank teller sorting bills
cards.body: A bank teller trained to spot counterfeit cash sees a mix of real and fake bills all day. Each time, they commit to a confidence: "I'm 95% sure this is real." A supervisor docks them hard for being loudly wrong and barely dings them for a cautious near-miss — that's exactly how BCE grades the discriminator.

cards.title: Grading one real, one fake image
cards.body: A real image has target = 1. If the discriminator says p = 0.8, its loss is -log(0.8) ≈ 0.22. A fake image has target = 0. If the discriminator says p = 0.3, its loss is -log(1-0.3) = -log(0.7) ≈ 0.36. Averaged over this tiny batch of two, the discriminator's loss is about 0.29.

cards.title: It's not a special GAN-only formula
cards.body: You might think GANs invented a brand-new loss for the discriminator. In reality, it's the same Binary Cross-Entropy from Unit 67 — the only twist is where the labels come from: 1 for real training images, 0 for the generator's freshly made fakes.

cards.title: Ordinary Classifier vs. GAN Discriminator
cards.body: Comparing what feeds into BCE loss:

left.text:
Labels come from a fixed dataset
e.g. cat vs. dog
Labels never change

right.text:
Labels come from the data's source
1 = real training image
0 = generator's fake output

exercises.prompt: A discriminator looks at a real image (target = 1) and outputs p = 0.95. Roughly how does this affect its BCE loss?

options.text: Very low loss — the prediction closely matches the true label with high confidence
Very high loss, because 0.95 isn't exactly 1.0
Zero loss always, for any real image
The loss is undefined for real images

exercises.explain: -log(0.95) ≈ 0.05 — a near-zero penalty for a confident, correct guess.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the discriminator's target label is 0 whenever it is shown a real training image.
exercises.explain: False. Real images get target 1; only the generator's fake images get target 0.

exercises.sentence: BCE penalizes the discriminator most when it is ___ wrong about whether an image is real or fake.
exercises.explain: The loss explodes as a wrong prediction's confidence approaches 100%, just like in Unit 67.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes what the discriminator's loss is computing?

options.text: How far its real/fake probability is from the true label (1 or 0), using -log(p) or -log(1-p)
The pixel-by-pixel difference between the fake image and a real image
The number of unique colors used in the generated image

exercises.explain: Correct — it's plain Binary Cross-Entropy on a probability, not a pixel comparison.

exercises.prompt: A discriminator is shown a fake image (target = 0) and outputs p = 0.9 — it was badly fooled. Which statement correctly describes its loss for this example?

options.text: The loss is very high, roughly -log(1-0.9) = -log(0.1) ≈ 2.30, because the discriminator was confidently wrong
The loss is zero because the discriminator made a confident prediction
The loss is negative because the prediction was above 0.5
The loss can't be computed because the image is fake, not real

exercises.explain: Exactly! Getting badly fooled by a good fake produces a large BCE loss — that's the signal that pushes the discriminator to get sharper. Next: how does the generator learn, when it never even sees the real images?
